coredata.dc:title: Development of a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on blue- and orange-emissive carbon dots for the determination of tartrazine in food products
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Safaei, Amir
Giyahban, Farshad
Ebrahimzadeh, Homeira
coredata.dc:description: Determination of tartrazine in food products is crucial due to its detrimental impacts on human health if it exceeds the maximum allowed intake limit. It was hypothesized that a ratiometric fluorescence sensor using blue- and orange-emissive carbon dots (B-O-CDs) would outperform single-emission-based sensors in sensitivity and reproducibility. A ratiometric probe was developed by mixing B-CDs and O-CDs, exhibiting emission peaks at 420 and 565 nm, differentially influenced by tartrazine. The fluorescence intensity ratio was used to quantify tartrazine with a detection limit of 64 nM, a linear range of 0.2–60 μM, recovery rates of 97.2–104.4 % and low relative standard deviation values (< 3.5 %) in food samples. The results confirm that the ratiometric approach enhances performance over single-emission-based sensors. The novel use of dual-emissive carbon dots and the inner filter effect offers a rapid, cost-effective, and reliable method for routine tartrazine monitoring in food products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent probe
Inner filter effect
Optical sensor
Synthetic food dyes
Food safety

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of B-CDs, O-CDs, and B-O-CDs
Optical characteristics of B-CDs, O-CDs, and B-O-CDs
Selectivity and anti-interference ability of B-O-CDs for tartrazine
Determination of tartrazine in food samples

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CIE chromaticity diagram of B-CDs and O-CDs mixed in different proportions between 9:1 and 1:9 (A). Fluorescence spectra of various mixing ratios of B-CDs and O-CDs under excitation at 350 nm (B). XRD patterns of B-CDs and O-CDs (C). TEM images of B-CDs, inset: histogram of the size distribution (D) and O-CDs, inset: histogram of the size distribution (E). FTIR spectra of B-CDs and O-CDs (F).

Emission spectra of B-CDs (A), O-CDs (B), and B-O-CDs (C) under different excitation wavelengths. Photographic images of B-CDs (left), O-CDs (center), and B-O-CDs (right) under the illumination of a 366 nm UV lamp (D). UV–Vis spectra of B-O-CDs and their precursors (E).
Effects of the different pH values (A) and incubation time (B) on the ratiometric sensing unit (B-O-CDs) response in the presence of 15 μM of tartrazine.

Fluorescence spectra of B-O-CDs in the presence of tartrazine with different concentrations ranging from 0 to 100 μM. (A). Photographic images of the B-O-CDs solution under the illumination of a 366 nm UV lamp with increasing tartrazine concentrations from 0 to 100 μM (B). Linear relationship between the response and tartrazine concentration in the concentration range of 0.2 to 60 μM. The response was defined as (I420/I565)0/(I420/I565), where I420 and I565 represent the fluorescence intensities at 420 nm and 565 nm, respectively, and the subscript 0 denotes the absence of tartrazine (C).
Response of B-O-CDs to tartrazine (10 μM) and various interfering species (A). Response of B-O-CDs to tartrazine (10 μM) in the absence and presence of interfering species (B).
UV–Vis absorption spectra of B-O-CDs, tartrazine, and their mixture (A). Normalized UV–Vis absorption spectrum of tartrazine and Normalized Fluorescence spectrum of B-O-CDs (B).
Fluorescence spectra of B-O-CDs in the presence of different spiking concentrations of tartrazine (0, 5, 25, and 50 μM) in orange juice (A), saffron tea bag (B), and jelly (C).
Determination of tartrazine in food samples using B-O-CDs (n = 3, technical replicates).
Comparison of the designed sensor with other CD-based fluorescence sensors for tartrazine determination.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Blue fluorescent carbon dots made from citric acid and tris base.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Carbon dots obtained from tomatoes as the precursor.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: g
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Carbon Dots obtained from Elaeagnus angustifolia as the precursor.

head.ce:title: Development of a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on blue- and orange-emissive carbon dots for the determination of tartrazine in food products

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry and Pollutants Sciences, Faculty of Chemistry and Petroleum Sciences, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Analytical Chemistry and Pollutants Sciences
Faculty of Chemistry and Petroleum Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry and Pollutants Sciences, Faculty of Chemistry and Petroleum Sciences, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Determination of tartrazine in food products is crucial due to its detrimental impacts on human health if it exceeds the maximum allowed intake limit. It was hypothesized that a ratiometric fluorescence sensor using blue- and orange-emissive carbon dots (B-O-CDs) would outperform single-emission-based sensors in sensitivity and reproducibility. A ratiometric probe was developed by mixing B-CDs and O-CDs, exhibiting emission peaks at 420 and 565 nm, differentially influenced by tartrazine. The fluorescence intensity ratio was used to quantify tartrazine with a detection limit of 64 nM, a linear range of 0.2–60 μM, recovery rates of 97.2–104.4 % and low relative standard deviation values (< 3.5 %) in food samples. The results confirm that the ratiometric approach enhances performance over single-emission-based sensors. The novel use of dual-emissive carbon dots and the inner filter effect offers a rapid, cost-effective, and reliable method for routine tartrazine monitoring in food products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tartrazine, a synthetic orange azo dye widely used as a food additive, has raised concerns due to potential adverse health effects associated with excessive consumption, including allergic reactions and organ toxicity (Chaudhari et al., 2024; Zoughi et al., 2021). Consequently, the accurate and reliable detection of tartrazine in food products is crucial for ensuring consumer safety (Yang et al., 2020). Tartrazine has been measured throughout the years by employing distinct, widely used analytical techniques, such as capillary electrophoresis, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay, and high-performance liquid chromatography (He & Du, 2023; Wang et al., 2019). Although these techniques display great performance in terms of accuracy and sensitivity, they have some disadvantages, including laborious sample preparation, use of toxic solvents, time-consuming procedures, and costly reagents, which restrict their widespread utilization (George et al., 2023; Yuan et al., 2023). Fluorescence-based sensors offer a promising alternative due to their simplicity, affordability, quick reaction time, and visual readout (Cheng et al., 2022; Peng et al., 2022).

Among various fluorescent materials, carbon dots (CDs) have gained significant attention due to their excellent optical properties, including tunable emission wavelength, ease of synthesis, and biocompatibility (Alafeef et al., 2024; Hebbar et al., 2023; Liu, Zhong, et al., 2024; Sun et al., 2024). These notable attributes make CDs a good choice for the fabrication of fluorescent probes (Rasheed, 2023).

However, at the moment, the majority of CDs-based fluorescence sensors only depend on variations in the intensity of a single emission. This approach tends to be vulnerable to errors due to background noises, matrix effects, instrument variables, and fluorescent probe concentration (Abd Elhaleem et al., 2024; Lin et al., 2023). These drawbacks were addressed using ratiometric fluorescence sensors, which allowed for a lower limit of detection (LOD) and a larger range of linearity (Chen et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2024). Analyte-induced changes in the intensity of two or more emission peaks serve as the basis for the design of ratiometric fluorescent probes (Park et al., 2020). An efficient internal referencing system is provided by the direct correlation between the analyte concentration and the ratios of fluorescence intensity at two distinct wavelengths. Because of this self-calibration capability, the ratiometric fluorescence approach can therefore enhance the sensitivity and accuracy of analysis in complex matrices (Gao et al., 2024; Liu, Tang, et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024).

Herein, to address the limitations of existing methods for tartrazine determination, we developed a ratiometric fluorescence sensing system (B-O-CDs) based on the physical mixing of blue-emissive carbon dots (B-CDs) and orange-emissive carbon dots (O-CDs) (Scheme 1 ). This approach took advantage of the unique optical properties of blue-emissive carbon dots (B-CDs) and orange-emissive carbon dots (O-CDs) to create a self-calibrating sensor that is potentially less susceptible to environmental interferences and instrumental variations. By combining the emission profiles of B-CDs and O-CDs, we aim to develop a method that is both sensitive and selective for determining tartrazine in complex food samples. The ratiometric design was expected to improve the accuracy and reproducibility of the measurements, making it more reliable than traditional single-emission sensors. This work represents, to the best of our knowledge, the first application of a dual-emissive carbon dot ratiometric sensor for tartrazine determination in food samples.

Glacial acetic acid, ortho-phosphoric acid 85 % w/w, boric acid, hydrochloric acid 37 % w/w (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium hydrogen carbonate (NaHCO3), potassium chloride (KCl), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2∙6H2O), magnesium nitrate hexahydrate (Mg(NO3)2∙6H2O), calcium oxalate monohydrate (CaC2O4∙H2O), citric acid, glucose, fructose, and ascorbic acid were purchased from Merck (Merck KGaA, Darmstadt, Germany). Tartrazine and Rhodamine B (RhB) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA). All the chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification. A bottle of pasteurized milk was bought from a local supermarket (Tehran, Iran). A stock solution of tartrazine was prepared in double-distilled water and stored at 4 °C. Also, a Britton-Robinson buffer was made by combining equal volumes of 0.04 M solutions of boric acid, acetic acid, and phosphoric acid. The pH of the buffer was subsequently adjusted to the desired value through the addition of 0.2 M NaOH or HCl.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a PerkinElmer LS 45 spectrometer (PerkinElmer, Waltham, MA, USA). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were obtained by a Rayleigh spectrophotometer UV-2601 (Rayleigh, Beijing, China). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Philips CM120, Philips, Amsterdam, Netherlands) was employed to assess the shape and particle size of the samples. Using the KBr pellet approach, a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet Nexus 470, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) was utilized to examine the functional groups of the CDs. The crystalline structure of the CDs was evaluated in the 2θ range between 10 and 50 degrees by a PANalytical X'PERT X-ray diffractometer equipped with a Cu Kα radiation source (PANalytical, Almelo, Netherlands). The pH values were adjusted using a digital pH meter (827 pH lab Metrohm, Metrohm, Herisau, Switzerland) equipped with a glass pH electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of B-CDs, O-CDs, and B-O-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: B-CDs were synthesized using a green precursor (Wang & Zhou, 2014). Briefly, 25 mL of milk was mixed with 20 mL of doubly distilled water and stirred for 30 min. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 2 h. Then, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe filter and lyophilized to obtain the B-CDs powder.

In order to synthesize O-CDs, 20 mL of ultrapure water was sonicated for 10 min to adequately dissolve 0.50 g of RhB powder. The resultant solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 4 h, followed by cooling down naturally to room temperature. To eliminate insoluble impurities, the supernatant was collected after employing 8000 rpm (6654×g) centrifugation for 10 min. To discard any remaining chemicals, resulting O-CDs were purified using a dialysis membrane bag (8–14 kDa) for 24 h. Ultrapure water (50 mL) was exchanged every 6 h. The O-CDs powder was obtained by freeze-drying (Li et al., 2024).

By adjusting the precursors ratio (B-CDs:O-CDs) to achieve the proper fluorescence color and emission, B-O-CDs were constructed. In short, the stock solutions of 400 μg mL−1 of B-CDs and 150 μg mL−1 of O-CDs were first diluted 10 times. Subsequently, the two diluted solutions were combined in different ratios, while the total volume remained constant in all the combinations. The best combination was identified by evaluation of the fluorescence spectra and used as the ratiometric fluorescent probe.

To assess the quantum yield (QY), quinine sulfate was chosen as the reference fluorophore. The QY of quinine sulfate diluted in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution is 0.54. The absorbance of samples was recorded at the wavelength of 350 nm. The following formula (Eq. (1)) was used to determine the QY:
(1)
ϕ X
=
ϕ ST
Grad X
Grad ST
η x 2
η ST 2
Where X and ST subscripts signify B-O-CDs and standard reference material (quinine sulfate), respectively. Φ represents the QY, η is the refractive index of the medium, and Grad denotes the slope of the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity of the samples versus their absorbance at 350 nm (Sistani & Shekarchizadeh, 2021).

To evaluate the sensitivity of the as-prepared probe, 200 μL of a series of standard tartrazine solutions with the concentration range of 0 to 100 μM were mixed with 200 μL of B-O-CDs. Next, 3.6 mL of Britton-Robinson buffer (pH = 7, 0.04 M) was added, and the resulting mixtures were incubated for 180 s at room temperature. Afterwards, the fluorescence spectra of the samples were recorded in optimum conditions. To assess the selectivity and anti-interference capability of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor, 200 μL of B-O-CDs was introduced to 200 μL of solutions of potential interfering ions and compounds, which might be present in food matrices. In the next step, 3.6 mL of Britton-Robinson buffer was added to the above mixture, and the fluorescence spectra were obtained in optimal conditions. All the measurements were carried out in triplicate to ensure the reproducibility of the results.

The applicability of the B-O-CDs probe was evaluated using food samples, including saffron tea bag, orange juice, and jelly which were bought from a local supermarket in Tehran (Iran). Each sample was prepared (n = 1, independent replicate) and analyzed in triplicate (n = 3, technical replicates), and the results are reported as the mean ± standard deviation (SD). The tea sample was prepared by immersing the tea bag in 2 mL of hot distilled water for 10 min, followed by the filtration of the tea extract (Zoughi et al., 2021). To prepare the orange juice sample, it was first shaken to assure its homogeneity and then diluted 10 times with distilled water. Following the sonication of the diluted mixture for 10 min, it was filtered by using a 0.22 μm syringe filter. Finally, the filtrate was collected and was kept in storage at 4 °C (Yang et al., 2020). The jelly sample was first broken up into small fragments. After that, 10 mL of distilled water was poured on 0.1 g of the weighted jelly sample and thoroughly stirred. The resultant mixture was centrifuged for 10 min at 12,000 rpm (14,972×g), and the supernatant was gathered for further analysis (Liu et al., 2021). The standard addition method was used to evaluate the recovery of tartrazine in these samples. Known concentrations of tartrazine were spiked into the prepared samples, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded under optimal conditions. The recovery rates and relative standard deviations (RSD) were calculated to assess the accuracy and precision of the method.

By adjusting the ratio of the two precursors, the optimization of B-O-CDs construction was carried out. The Commission on Illumination (CIE) chromaticity diagram (Fig. 1A) and fluorescence spectra were used to determine the proper ratio of B-CDs:O-CDs, which ranged from 9:1 to 1:9. All samples exhibited two emission peaks in their fluorescence spectrum at 420 and 565 nm, as shown in Fig. 1B. The ratio of the fluorescence intensity at these wavelengths altered as the ratio of the two precursors changed. Whereas the intensity of the emission at 420 nm rose with the ratio varying from 1:9 to 9:1, the intensity at 565 nm decreased. The equilibrium of the fluorescence intensity appeared at the ratio of 3:1, with a light purple color. Furthermore, the CIE demonstrated the optimal ratio of 3:1, allowing a wider range of change in the fluorescence color upon the addition of analyte. The ratio of 3:1 was therefore selected as the optimum ratio.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns for B-CDs and O-CDs are illustrated in Fig. 1C. Two broad peaks at 2θ values of 20.32o and 21o can be observed for B-CDs and O-CDs, respectively, which corresponded to the (002) plane of graphite and represented the amorphous structure of the CDs (Zhang et al., 2023). Average particle size and morphology of the nanoparticles were determined by the TEM images (Figs. 1D and E). The images showed that B-CDs and O-CDs were quasi-spherical and well-dispersed and had the average particle sizes of 3.48 and 2.34 nm, respectively (inset of Figs. 1D and E).

FTIR spectroscopy was employed to analyze the functional groups of the CDs. As depicted in Fig. 1F, B-CDs exhibited a broad absorption band at 3388 cm−1 related to the stretching vibrations of the OH and NH bonds. Also, the peaks at 2931, 1644, and 1402 cm−1 correspond to the CH, CO, and CN stretching vibrations, respectively (Wang & Zhou, 2014). Furthermore, the peaks at 1072 and 779 cm−1 are the result of the stretching vibrations of the CO bonds and bending vibrations of the CH bonds, in that order. The FTIR spectrum of O-CDs showed an absorption band at 3423 cm−1 which is related to the stretching vibrations of the OH and NH bonds. Besides, the peaks at 2974, 1647, and 1473 cm−1 originated from the stretching vibrations of the CH, CO, and CO bonds, respectively. In addition, the peaks appeared at the wavenumbers of 1410 and 1074 cm−1 were due to the stretching vibrations of the CN and CO. Finally, bending vibrations of CH bonds resulted in a peak that can be observed at 823 cm−1 (Li et al., 2024).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characteristics of B-CDs, O-CDs, and B-O-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of B-O-CDs and their precursors have been shown through their fluorescence and absorption spectra. The fluorescence spectra of B-CDs under different excitation wavelengths are demonstrated in Fig. 2A. What stands out from the graph is that the emission of these CDs manifested excitation-dependent behavior. In other words, following the increase in the excitation wavelength, the emission peak displaced to the longer wavelengths (bathochromic shift). The maximum emission of B-CDs was obtained under excitation at 330 nm. Unlike B-CDs, O-CDs exhibited excitation-independent behavior in the examined range (Fig. 2B). The emission peak of O-CDs was located at 565 nm, and the maximum emission of these nanoparticles occurred under excitation at 350 nm. Additionally, the emission behavior of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor (B-O-CDs) was investigated under excitation at a range of wavelengths (Fig. 2C). The excitation wavelength of 350 nm was selected as optimal, as it maximized the emission intensity at 565 nm (O-CDs) while maintaining a significant emission at 420 nm (B-CDs), leading to a balanced intensity ratio between the two peaks. By decreasing the excitation wavelength to the shorter wavelengths, the emission peak at 565 nm declined significantly. Furthermore, increasing the excitation wavelength to above 350 nm resulted in a fall in both of the emission intensities at 420 and 565 nm. Therefore, under excitation at 350 nm, two independent emissions from B-O-CDs could be observed that originated from their precursors. This observation corroborated the proper construction of the ratiometric sensing unit and splendid stability of B-CDs and O-CDs following their physical mixing.

The photographic images of B-CDs, O-CDs, and B-O-CDs under the illumination of a 366 nm UV lamp are demonstrated in Fig. 2D. It can clearly be seen that under these circumstances, B-CDs, O-CDs, and B-O-CDs appeared blue, orange, and light purple, respectively.

To investigate optical features further, UV–Vis absorption spectra of B-O-CDs and their precursors were recorded (Fig. 2E). The UV–Vis spectrum of B-CDs matched a previous report (Wang & Zhou, 2014). Due to the electronic transitions in B-CDs, a peak at 280 nm was seen in its UV–Vis spectrum, which related to the π–π* transitions of the CC bonds. In addition, an absorption tail stretching to approximately 400 nm can be ascribed to the n–π* transitions of the CO and NH bonds and verified the presence of the doped nitrogen in the CDs structure. The significant absorptions at 255 and 350 nm in the UV–Vis spectrum of O-CDs originated from the π–π* transitions of the CC bonds and n–π* transitions of the CO and CN bonds, respectively. Also, opening of the amide ring led to an absorption peak at 550 nm (Tang et al., 2022). Finally, the UV–Vis spectrum of B-O-CDs had all the significant absorption peaks of their precursors, which further confirmed appropriate physical mixing of B-CDs and O-CDs.
The QY of B-O-CDs was measured relative to quinine sulfate using Eq. (1). The calculated QY value was determined to be 22.63 % (Supplementary Fig. 1).

The photoluminescence stability of B-O-CDs was investigated to ascertain their potential in real applications. The effects of pH, salt, temperature, and UV illumination time on the fluorescence intensity of B-O-CDs were studied.

Firstly, the ratio of the fluorescence intensities at the wavelengths of 420 and 565 nm (I420/I565) for B-O-CDs was measured in different pH values (4–11). As portrayed in Supplementary Fig. 2A, this ratio did not experience a significant change in the studied range. Nevertheless, the ratio in the vicinity of the neutral pH values demonstrated slightly larger magnitudes. In general, the designed ratiometric sensor exhibited excellent stability in a range of pH values.

The I420/I565 ratio in different NaCl concentrations ranging from 0 to 1 M was measured (Supplementary Fig. 2B). By raising the NaCl concentration, this ratio remained nearly constant, which showcased the high photoluminescence stability of the sensor in high ionic strength environments.

Furthermore, the influence of temperature on the fluorescence was analyzed. Generally, in high temperatures, intermolecular collisions happen more frequently, and the possibility that molecules lose their excess energy through non-radiative pathways increases. Therefore, it is probable that the fluorescence intensity dwindles as the temperature increases (Yu et al., 2012). As illustrated in Supplementary Fig. 2C, the I420/I565 ratio increased steadily by altering the temperature from 25 to 55 °C. This happened due to the fact that the fluorescence intensity at 565 nm declined at a slower pace than that at 420 nm, which led to the greater ratio.

The good anti-photobleaching performance of B-O-CDs was confirmed by exposing them to direct UV light at 366 nm for 30 min (Supplementary Fig. 2D). The intensities ratio did not change substantially with increasing time and fell only around 15 % after 30 min of direct UV radiation.
The above results corroborated the optical and environmental stability of B-O-CDs, making them a promising fluorescent probe in practical applications.

To achieve the best performance for sensing tartrazine, factors that affect the efficiency of the ratiometric sensor, including the pH and incubation time, were scrutinized and optimized. A tartrazine concentration of 15 μM was selected for optimization experiments as preliminary tests showed it was in the linear range of the method, resulting in a strong signal well above background noise. This ensured representative optimization within the sensor's working range.

With regard to the pH optimization, the performance of the sensing unit in Britton-Robinson buffer in a range of pH values between 4 and 9 for sensing tartrazine was evaluated. The ratio of the fluorescence intensity in the absence of tartrazine (I420/I565)0 divided by this ratio in the presence of tartrazine (I420/I565) was considered as the response. As represented in Fig. 3A, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor almost manifested the same response in the examined range of pH values, which verified the performance of the ratiometric system. While the changes in the (I420/I565)0/(I420/I565) ratio shown in Fig. 3A may appear small, they represent significant changes in the underlying fluorescence intensities. This is because the plotted ratio reflects the change in the ratio of fluorescence intensities, not the absolute fluorescence intensity. Even small variations in this ratio can indicate meaningful changes in the individual fluorescence emissions at 420 nm and 565 nm. However, this ratio, which serves as the response, did not vary enormously in different pH values, which is a result of the self-correction capability of the ratiometric sensing unit. This characteristic made the sensor robust against variations in various environmental factors. Nonetheless, given the higher response, pH = 7 was selected as the optimal pH for the subsequent analyses.

Furthermore, the measurement of the ratiometric sensor response was utilized to pinpoint the optimum incubation time (Fig. 3B). The response of B-O-CDs to tartrazine surged following the addition of the analyte and reached its maximum value in 3 min. Then, the response remained constant throughout the remainder of the investigated period. This demonstrated that the reaction between B-O-CDs and tartrazine equilibrated in 3 min. Therefore, the incubation time of 3 min was chosen as the optimal time for further analyses, which proved the ability of B-O-CDs to respond to tartrazine rapidly.

To determine the LOD and obtain the calibration curve and linear range of the ratiometric sensor for tartrazine, the fluorescence spectra of B-O-CDs were recorded in the optimal conditions and in the presence of tartrazine concentrations ranging from 0 to 100 μM (Fig. 4A). As the concentration of tartrazine increased, the fluorescence intensities at the wavelengths of 420 and 565 nm declined, and a greater decrease in the intensity at 420 nm than that at 565 nm provided the possibility of the development of a ratiometric approach. These changes in the fluorescence intensity were intuitively shown in Fig. 4B. As depicted in Fig. 4C, a good linear relationship was observed between the response ((I420/I565)0/(I420/I565)) and the tartrazine concentration in the range of 0.2–60 μM. The calibration equation was equal to (I420/I565)0/(I420/I565) = 0.02136x + 0.99876 with a coefficient of determination (R 2) of 0.99265 (x represents the concentration of tartrazine in μM). The LOD of this ratiometric probe was calculated to be 64 nM using the formula 3.3S b/m (where S b is the standard deviation of I420/I565 for B-O-CDs (n = 6), and m signifies the slope of the calibration curve). The LOD for this probe is significantly lower than the World Health Organization's maximum allowed daily tartrazine intake of 7.5 mg kg−1 body weight and the European Parliament and Council Directive 94/36/EC's permissible range of 50–500 mg kg−1 for food products (Chaudhari et al., 2024).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference ability of B-O-CDs for tartrazine
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of B-O-CDs for tartrazine was investigated by evaluation of their fluorescence spectra in response to tartrazine (10 μM) and some other interfering species, which might be present in food matrices. These species included ionic species (K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cl−, C2O4 2−, NO3 −, and HCO3 −) and molecular species (glucose, citric acid, ascorbic acid, and fructose). The concentration of each interfering species was 100 times that of tartrazine. As shown in Fig. 5A, the as-prepared ratiometric sensor had high selectivity for tartrazine and virtually did not respond to the other species. Here, (I420/I565)0/(I420/I565) is the response, where (I420/I565)0 is the ratio of fluorescence intensity in the absence of tartrazine (or interfering species), and (I420/I565) is the ratio of fluorescence intensity in the presence of tartrazine (or interfering species). Furthermore, the anti-interference capability of B-O-CDs in the determination of tartrazine in the presence of the interfering species was studied (Fig. 5B). The results showed that B-O-CDs had a great performance even when the other species co-existed in the solution. This makes the ratiometric sensing system an appealing option for sensing tartrazine.

The quenching mechanism of fluorescence of B-O-CDs by tartrazine was surveyed using UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–Vis absorption spectra for tartrazine, B-O-CDs, and their mixture (B-O-CDs + tartrazine) were recorded (Fig. 6A). No additional peaks other than those associated with the precursors and tartrazine were observed, and no hypsochromic or bathochromic shifts with respect to them were detected in the UV–Vis spectrum of B-O-CDs + tartrazine. It could be concluded that no new complexes were formed and the reason behind the fluorescence quenching was not static quenching (Wang et al., 2021).

Further investigations revealed that the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of tartrazine had a huge overlap with the fluorescence emission spectrum of B-O-CDs (Fig. 6B). This suggested that the fluorescence quenching mechanism might have been the inner filter effect (IFE), in which the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of a quencher overlaps with the excitation/emission spectrum of a fluorophore (Qiu et al., 2023). Unlike Förster resonance energy transfer, which depends on the distance between the quencher and fluorophore, IFE is a distance-independent quenching mechanism. Besides, IFE does not have any influences on the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorophore (Zhang et al., 2022).

The versatility of B-O-CDs, as a fluorescent probe, stems from the IFE mechanism. The simplicity of this mechanism eliminates the need for complex surface modifications or specific binding interactions, making B-O-CDs adaptable to a wide range of contaminants. The broad applicability of this strategy is further enhanced by the tunable optical properties of B-O-CDs, which can be tailored during synthesis to match the absorption profiles of various analytes (Chen et al., 2018; Qiu et al., 2023). Furthermore, the B-O-CDs probe exhibits potential for simultaneous detection of multiple analytes. By leveraging machine learning algorithms such as linear discriminant analysis and partial least squares regression, the unique fluorescence quenching patterns caused by different analytes could be decoded, enabling the identification and quantification of multiple contaminants in a single assay (Koushkestani et al., 2024; Pesaran et al., 2024).

In addition, the reason behind the marginal decrease in the fluorescence intensity at 565 nm with raising tartrazine concentration is that tartrazine has absorbance at 350 nm (Fig. 6A), which is the wavelength used for the excitation of B-O-CDs. Therefore, fewer photons (i.e., less energy) interacted with the ratiometric probe, and as a consequence, its fluorescence emission at 565 nm quenched.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of tartrazine in food samples

ce:section.ce:para: The comparison of different parameters of the designed ratiometric sensing unit (B-O-CDs) with other fluorescence-based sensors that have been used for tartrazine detection is illustrated in Table 2 . To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study that has utilized the ratiometric strategy for the determination of tartrazine. Although LOD and RSD values for the current method are comparable to those of the other methods, its linear range is wider compared to them. Moreover, B-O-CDs exhibited a good QY value, and the precursors used for the construction of them are quite cost-effective. Specifically, the synthesis of B-O-CDs relies on readily available materials (milk and Rhodamine B). This is in contrast with some other sensing probes that utilize more costly precursors. The low cost and ease of synthesis of B-O-CDs provide an economic advantage for this approach. Eventually, it is worth mentioning that the response time of this sensor is only 3 min, which is suitable for the rapid detection of tartrazine.

Briefly, a ratiometric fluorescent probe was successfully constructed from the physical mixing of B-CDs and O-CDs and applied for the detection of tartrazine. The employment of the ratiometric strategy led to an increase in the sensitivity and reproducibility of the sensing process. The morphology, chemical composition, optical properties, and photoluminescence stability of B-O-CDs were thoroughly scrutinized for further applications. Furthermore, UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra suggested that the mechanism of sensing is probably the IFE. The as-prepared sensor had a wide linear range between 0.2 and 60 μM and a low LOD (64 nM), which is much lower than the maximum allowed level of tartrazine usage for humans according to the World Health Organization. In addition, the suitable recovery values in the range of 97.2–104.4 % with the RSD values of less than 3.5 % in food matrices indicated the high resistance of the sensor in different environmental conditions and its potential to be used in real applications. Eventually, it should be mentioned that this study provided a new insight for the construction of CD-based ratiometric fluorescent probes, which have huge importance in food safety surveillance and human health protection.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Amir Safaei: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Farshad Giyahban: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Formal analysis. Homeira Ebrahimzadeh: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Graph of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance for quinine sulfate and B-O-CDs for quantum yield (QY) calculation. Effects of pH, NaCl concentration, temperature, and UV illumination time on fluorescence intensity ratio.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2025.143582.
